ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Korean kalguksu with pork belly. FOODON_03302050 (noodle soup food product) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Korean kalguksu with samgyeopsal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of handmade kalguksu noodles in pork bone broth topped with crispy pan-fried pork belly slices and scallion oil, as typically served at a Korean restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, belly, raw (FDC ID 168289); USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, egg, cooked (FDC ID 172030); USDA FoodData Central — Soup, pork bone broth (estimated from pork broth, FDC ID 172940); Korean National Food Composition Database (NFCD) — Kalguksu reference values
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Korean Rural Development Administration (RDA) NFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish (Crispy Samgyeopsal Kkal Guksu with Pork Bone Broth). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for pork belly, wheat noodles, and pork bone broth, cross-referenced with Korean NFCD kalguksu reference values. Approximate dish composition assumed: 150 g cooked wheat noodles, 120 g crispy pork belly, 250 g pork bone broth, 30 g scallion oil and aromatics. Per-100g values derived from this 550 g total serving estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (GI ~52); Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Crispy Samgyeopsal Kkal Guksu. GI of 52 is assigned based on the GI of boiled wheat noodles (kalguksu-type), which is the dominant glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The pork belly and broth components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Source: Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables of GI and GL Values; wheat noodles boiled GI ~52 (low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 72.6) / 100 ≈ 37.8, rounded to 38.0. Carbohydrate per serving = 13.2 g/100g × 550 g = 72.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork bone broth and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid the broth, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 2,310 mg per full serving) substantially exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from wheat noodles and high fat content from pork belly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious of the moderate-to-high glycemic load (GL ≈ 38 per serving) driven by the wheat noodle component, and should monitor portion size; the high fat content from crispy pork belly may also contribute to postprandial insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from pork belly and high sodium from pork bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high saturated fat content (approximately 4.3 g per 100 g, estimated 23.7 g per serving) from crispy pork belly and its high sodium load from the pork bone broth, both of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Samgyeopsal Kkal Guksu with Pork Bone Broth is a Korean noodle soup dish featuring handmade knife-cut wheat noodles (kalguksu) served in a rich, slow-simmered pork bone broth (similar to gamjatang or seolleongtang-style stock), topped with slices of pan-fried or deep-fried crispy pork belly (samgyeopsal) and finished with fragrant scallion oil. The dish combines the chewy, silky texture of fresh-made noodles with the deeply savoury, collagen-rich broth and the contrasting crunch of the crispy pork belly. Scallion oil adds aromatic depth and a mild sweetness. It is typically served with Korean side dishes (banchan). Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense dish with significant fat content from the pork belly and moderate carbohydrate from the wheat noodles. The pork bone broth contributes collagen, gelatin, and minerals but also a high sodium load. The dish is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets. Clinically, the high sodium content (estimated ~2,300 mg per serving) is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management, while the moderate glycemic load (~38 per serving) and high saturated fat content require careful portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or dyslipidaemia. This dish is served at Korean restaurants in Singapore, including Ajumma's at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No exact match for this specific restaurant dish was found in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, Korean NFCD). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for pork belly, wheat noodles, and pork bone broth, cross-referenced with Korean NFCD kalguksu reference values. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled wheat noodles, GI 52) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated, not directly measured from this dish. Actual values at Ajumma's - Funan may differ depending on recipe, portion size, and cooking method. Follow-up with restaurant-specific nutritional disclosure is recommended. 2. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100g is based on typical pork bone broth sodium levels from USDA FoodData Central and Korean NFCD kalguksu references; actual restaurant sodium may be higher. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles per Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables of GI and GL Values; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 4. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record; however, this dish is a mixed noodle soup and could also be classified under 'Mixed'. The input classification is retained as the dominant protein source is pork. 5. Halal status is false as pork is a primary ingredient.
